title: The Brave New World Problem: Why Abundance Without Ownership Is a Trap

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
The coming decade will produce staggering abundance. A billion robots will manufacture goods. Self-driving cars will slash transportation costs. AI will generate infinite entertainment, education, medical advice, and legal counsel at near-zero marginal cost. Michael Saylor doesn't dispute any of this. His concern isn't scarcity of stuff — it's that people confuse access to abundance with actual prosperity. Having unlimited free music, entertainment, and consumer goods does not make you wealthy. It makes you comfortable, dependent, and ultimately vulnerable.

Saylor draws on Aldous Huxley's Brave New World to frame the risk: the dystopia isn't Orwellian surveillance but rather infinite entertainment, sex, drugs, and stimulation that pacifies entire populations. You can already watch TikToks 80 hours a week, stream unlimited Spotify, scroll infinite YouTube Shorts. The debate isn't whether there will be enough — there will be too much. Too much food, too much sugar, too much stimulation, too much of everything that can be manufactured or digitized. The differentiator between civilizations will be how they respond to this deluge.

The political response, Saylor predicts, will be a socialist wave of entitlements. Universal healthcare, universal basic income, universal housing, universal transportation — the list of things the state is expected to provide will only grow. He traces an alarming progression he's witnessed in his own lifetime: from no drugs offered in college, to free healthcare for students, to prescription medications in high school, to the normalization of unlimited state-sponsored pharmaceuticals for teenagers without parental consent. Each step seemed reasonable in isolation; the cumulative trajectory is something no one explicitly voted for.

The deeper philosophical point is that the human experience may not align with abundance at all. Saylor argues that the natural order is scarcity and Darwinian competition, and that when this breaks down, you get artificiality — like a curated garden that requires constant intervention and collapses the moment the gardener disappears. Abundance without purpose, struggle, and ownership creates fragility, not resilience.

The actionable implication is clear: don't mistake access to abundance for ownership of value. The person consuming infinite free content on a government stipend is not in the same position as the person who owns the network distributing that content, or the scarce digital asset underpinning the financial system. Stake your claim in ownership — of assets, networks, and protocols — before the window closes and the entitlement state decides your share for you.
